name: Firbolg
description: The Fir Bolg (also spelled Firbolg or Fir Bholg) were one of the early peoples to inhabit Ireland, traditionally believed to have arrived before the Tuatha Dé Danann. Their name translates to "men of bags" or "bag men," possibly referring to their servitude to the Greeks, where they were forced to carry bags of soil. According to some accounts, they were descendants of Nemglan, a son of a king of Greece, who led his people to Ireland. The Fir Bolg are depicted as skilled builders and farmers, responsible for dividing Ireland into provinces and establishing a rudimentary legal system. They are often associated with the construction of stone forts and other ancient structures. Their reign in Ireland was brought to an end by the arrival of the Tuatha Dé Danann, who defeated them in the First Battle of Mag Tuired. Some Fir Bolg are said to have fled to remote islands, while others integrated into the Tuatha Dé Danann society. They represent a significant layer in the complex tapestry of Irish mythological history, embodying themes of labor, resilience, and displacement. They are remembered for their contributions to the land and their ultimate defeat by a more powerful, magical race. The Fir Bolg stand as a reminder of the cyclical nature of power and the constant flux of populations in ancient Irish narratives. Their legacy persists in place names and folklore, connecting the modern landscape to its ancient past. Their story is one of early settlement, societal organization, and eventual subjugation, a common motif in many origin myths.
subtitle: Ancient inhabitants of Ireland
appearance: The Fir Bolg are generally described as being of average height and build, though some accounts suggest they were shorter and stockier than the Tuatha Dé Danann. They were known for their strength and endurance, honed by years of hard labor. Their appearance is not heavily emphasized in most myths, but they are often depicted as being rough and weathered, reflecting their connection to the land and their history of servitude.
behavior: The Fir Bolg are portrayed as hard-working, practical, and organized. They valued order and stability, as evidenced by their division of the land into provinces and their establishment of a legal system. They were initially resistant to the arrival of the Tuatha Dé Danann, leading to conflict, but eventually, some integrated into the new society. Their behavior reflects a deep connection to the land and a strong sense of community.

keyMyths.title: The First Battle of Mag Tuired
keyMyths.description: The Fir Bolg were defeated by the Tuatha Dé Danann in the First Battle of Mag Tuired, marking the end of their reign in Ireland. This battle highlights the disparity in power between the two groups, with the Tuatha Dé Danann possessing superior weapons and magical abilities.

keyMyths.title: Division of Ireland
keyMyths.description: The Fir Bolg are credited with dividing Ireland into five provinces: Ulster, Munster, Leinster, Connacht, and Meath. This division established a framework for governance and land management that influenced later Irish society.

sources.author: Geoffrey Keating
sources.work: Foras Feasa ar Éirinn (The History of Ireland)
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Ireland, providing details about the Fir Bolg and their role in the island's early history.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Ireland, providing details about the Fir Bolg and their role in the island's early history.
